query: what is multi threading in OS
answer:
**[FROM NOTES]**  
> “On the other hand, a **thread** represents a single flow of control within a process. A system supports **multi‑threading** when it can execute different threads in parallel within a process.” 【Sandeep Bhowmik - Cloud Computing.pdf | Page: 370】

**[EXPLANATION]**  
In an operating system, multithreading is the capability to run multiple threads—each a separate sequence of execution—concurrently inside the same process. This allows a program to perform several tasks at the same time, improving throughput and responsiveness by sharing the process’s resources (memory, file handles, etc.) among its threads.

19.14.1  Thread Programming
High performance computation system focuses on delivering better output during computation. 
High throughput  in computation is achieved by allowing concurrency through multi-processing 
and multi-threading. A process represents a program in execution. In multi-processing, multiple 
processes are executed in parallel at a single machine. Such system is meant to support multi-
tasking. On the other hand, a thread represents a single flow of control within a process. A system 
supports multi-threading when it can execute different threads in parallel within a process.

Aneka
For high-end requirements, performance of executing multi-threaded applications on a 
single multi-core system (systems having two or more processing units, known as cores and 
which is generally attached as a single component) becomes insufficient. In such cases, the 
distributed execution of application is the only solution. For this purpose, an application can 
be decomposed into several units.
In Aneka, multi-thread programming is implemented over cloud, using Thread Programming 
Model. In this model, threads are treated as distributed threads being known as Aneka thread. 
Aneka threads follow the principle of local threads which can be executed over distributed 
system architecture. Aneka schedules the executions of threads efficiently while creation and 
control of the threads

query: differentiate a thread and a process
answer:
**[FROM NOTES]**  
- “A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. It is a lightweight process. Multiple threads can exist within the same process, sharing the same memory space” 【PPT-Unit3b.pdf | Page: 6】.  
- “Share the same memory space and resources of the process… More efficient than processes due to lower context‑switching overhead” 【PPT-Unit3b.pdf | Page: 6】.  
- “All threads have exactly the same address space, which means that they also share the same global variables… Every thread can access every virtual address, one thread can read, write, or even completely wipe out another thread’s stack” 【PPT-Unit3b.pdf | Page: 8】.  
- “Threads within the same process share the same memory space but have separate execution contexts, such as the program counter and stack” 【PPT-Unit3b.pdf | Page: 22】.  

**[EXPLANATION]**  
- **Thread:** the smallest schedulable unit; lives inside a process; shares the process’s address space, global variables, and other resources; has its own stack, program counter, and registers; incurs low overhead for creation and context switching.  
- **Process:** (not explicitly described in the notes) is an independent execution entity that owns its own separate memory address space and resources; processes do **not** share memory by default, making them heavier to create and switch between.  

*Partially found in notes* – the notes provide detailed characteristics of threads but do not contain a direct description of processes, so the process side of the comparison is inferred from general OS concepts.

contexts.text:
Introduction: Thread
• A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process. 
It is a lightweight process. Multiple threads can exist within 
the same process, sharing the same memory space
Characteristics
• Share the same memory space and resources of the process.
• More efficient than processes due to lower context-
switching overhead.
• Easier and more efficient for communication within the 
same process.

Introduction to Threads and 
Remote Procedure Calls (RPC)
• Threads: A thread is the smallest unit of a process that can 
be scheduled and executed by the operating system.
• Threads within the same process share the same memory 
space but have separate execution contexts, such as the 
program counter and stack.
• Threads can be implemented at the user level or kernel 
level:
• User-Level Threads: Managed by a user-level library, invisible to 
the kernel.
• Kernel-Level Threads: Managed directly by the operating system.

Introduction: Thread
• While one thread is blocked, another thread in the same 
process can run, in exactly the same way that when one 
process blocks, another process in the same machine can run
• Different threads in a process are not quite as independent 
as different processes.
• All threads have exactly the same address space, which 
means that they also share the same global variables.
• Every thread can access every virtual address, one thread 
can read, write, or even completely wipe out another 
thread’s stack

query: what is memory in Computer Science
answer:
**[FROM NOTES]**  
- “Non‑uniform memory access (NUMA) is a **computer memory design** used in multiprocessing systems where memory access time depends on the memory location relative to the processor accessing it.”【PPT-Unit5.pdf | Page: 29】  
- “Each processor has its own **local memory** and can also access memory from other processors over a high‑speed interconnect.”【PPT-Unit5.pdf | Page: 29】  
- “However, accessing local memory is faster than accessing remote memory due to differences in latency.”【PPT-Unit5.pdf | Page: 29】  
- “In addition to the physical RAM installed in your computer, most modern operating systems allow your computer to use a **virtual memory** system. Virtual memory allows our computer to use part of our secondary memory as extra memory.”【OS_Notes_Complete_by_Prof._Bacchanidhi.pdf | Page: 9】

**[EXPLANATION]**  
Memory in Computer Science refers to the hardware and logical structures that store data and program instructions for a computer. It includes **primary memory (RAM)**, which is directly accessed by the CPU, and **secondary storage** that can be used as **virtual memory** to extend the apparent size of RAM. In multiprocessor systems, memory may be organized as **NUMA**, where each processor has its own local memory and can also reach remote memory, but with non‑uniform access times.  

*Partially found in notes* – the provided excerpts describe memory’s role and organization but do not give a single textbook definition.

contexts.text:
Non-uniform memory access 
(NUMA)
• Non-uniform memory access (NUMA) is a computer memory design used in multiprocessing 
systems where memory access time depends on the memory location relative to the 
processor accessing it. 
• In a NUMA architecture, multiple processors (or nodes) are connected to a shared memory 
pool. 
• Each processor has its own local memory and can also access memory from other 
processors over a high-speed interconnect. 
• However, accessing local memory is faster than accessing remote memory due to 
differences in latency.
• The primary motivation behind NUMA is to alleviate memory access bottlenecks in large-
scale multiprocessing systems. As the number of processors increases in a system, the 
contention for accessing a shared memory pool also increases, leadin

• IVY-System: These systems are built on top of multicomputers that is, 
processors connected by a specialized message-passing network, workstations 
on a LAN, or similar designs. The essential element here is that no processor 
can directly access any other processor's memory. Such systems are 
sometimes called NORMA (NO Remote Memory Access) systems to contrast 
them with NUMA systems.
• At hardware level memory access of other CPU is not possible in NORM.
• Chip design is different.
• In NUMA, at hardware level CPU and memory are integrated (bundled). The 
memory access of CPU for local memory is faster compared to the memory 
access of other CPU, therefore NUMA is called non-uniform memory accessed.

> “it is a sys software that acts as an intermediany/interface blw a user of a compute2 comp . hardware . computer Sys . Hardware + Software OS is always Structure of Computer System . stored in Secondary Memory such as User Hard Disks/SSDS.” 【OS_Notes_Complete_by_Prof._Bacchanidhi.pdf | Page: 2】

**[EXPLANATION]**  
The operating system (OS) is the system software that sits between users (and their applications) and the computer’s hardware. It provides an interface for users to interact with the machine, manages hardware resources, and is itself stored on secondary storage (e.g., hard disks or SSDs).

Software Concepts
• Two types of OSs for multiple CPU systems
• Loosely-coupled
• A group of PCs, each has its own CPU, its own memory, its own HDD, its 
own OS, but shares some resources, such as printers, databases, over a 
LAN/MAN/WAN.
• All the PCs are independent of one another.
• All the PCs are connected via some network
• Tightly-coupled 
• All CPUs are at one place
• The microprocessors execute in parallel

answer:
**[FROM NOTES]**  

- “When CPU switches to another proces, the system must save the state of old process and load the said state for new proces vic context switch.” 【OS_Notes_Complete_by_Prof._Bacchanidhi.pdf | Page: 16】  

- “context of < proces represented in PCB.” 【OS_Notes_Complete_by_Prof._Bacchanidhi.pdf | Page: 16】  

- “context switching is … Time dependent on hardware support.” 【OS_Notes_Complete_by_Prof._Bacchanidhi.pdf | Page: 16】  

- “Process Control – context Block (saves process)” 【OS_Notes_Complete_by_Prof._Bacchanidhi.pdf | Page: 12】

**[EXPLANATION]**  
Context switching is the operating‑system operation performed when the CPU stops executing one process (or thread) and starts executing another. To do this, the OS saves the current process’s execution state (registers, program counter, etc.) into its Process Control Block (PCB) and then restores the saved state of the next process from its PCB. The speed of a context switch depends on the underlying hardware support.
